claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children that I'm asking about - Alex, who is 6, and Blake, who is 3. They both live with me and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have two children, Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3), who both live with you.

Now I need to ask: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And just to confirm: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now I need to ask: **Are either Alex or Blake in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or have they been for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has been in hospital for a while now - it's been a few months actually. Alex is fine though, no issues there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. I need to understand Blake's situation a bit more. You mentioned Blake has been in hospital for a few months.

**Can you tell me more specifically how many weeks Blake has been in hospital continuously? And are you regularly spending money on Blake during this hospital stay (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for them)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 13 weeks now that Blake has been in hospital. And unfortunately, I haven't been able to spend money on them while they've been in there - it's been really difficult financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I understand this must be a difficult time for you and your family.

Just one more question to make sure I have all the details: **Are Alex or Blake fostered or adopted, or are they your biological children? And is there any involvement from your local council paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're my own children - not fostered or adopted. And no, there's no involvement from the council paying for anything.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment

Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment for each of your children:

### **Alex (age 6): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You qualify for Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, you're responsible for them, and there are no circumstances affecting eligibility. You can claim Child Benefit for Alex.

### **Blake (age 3): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE (currently)**
Unfortunately, you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Here's why:

Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week threshold. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected when a child is in hospital for more than 12 weeks. After the 12-week limit, you can only continue receiving Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on them while they're in hospital (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, etc.). Since you've indicated you haven't been able to spend money on Blake during their hospital stay, the exception doesn't apply.

### What You Can Do

- **For Alex**: You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit
- **For Blake**: Once Blake leaves hospital, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them again. If your financial circumstances change and you're able to start regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital, you should contact HMRC as this might change your eligibility

I hope Blake recovers soon and that this information helps you understand your situation.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old, which is under 16. Alex lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Alex. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is not in hospital, care, or residential accommodation. There are no special circumstances affecting eligibility. Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16, and normally the claimant would qualify. However, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks continuously. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments might be affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. After 12 weeks, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless there is an exception - for example, if they regularly spend money on the child while in hospital. The claimant has stated they have not been able to spend money on Blake during the hospital stay. Therefore, as Blake has exceeded the 12-week limit and no exception applies, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). While the claimant would normally qualify for both children, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, exceeding the 12-week threshold. Since the claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake during the hospital stay, the exception does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex only, but not for Blake while Blake remains in hospital beyond 12 weeks without the claimant making regular financial contributions.

Therefore, as Blake has exceeded the 12-week limit and no exception applies, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
